lesson: Use mp.Pool(processes=N).map(func, iterable) to parallelize CPU-bound work; func must be a module-level, picklable function, not a closure or lambda.
Use pool.imap(func, iterable) instead of pool.map for streaming: it returns a lazy iterator immediately and preserves input order, unlike map's full-list block.
Tune chunksize in Pool.imap: chunksize=1 gives fine-grained load balancing but high IPC overhead; larger chunksize cuts overhead at the cost of balance.
Use pool.imap_unordered when task order doesn't matter: results arrive in completion order, letting downstream consumers start on fast-finishing items sooner than imap allows.

Share mutable state across Pool workers with mp.Value/mp.Array plus Pool(initializer=..., initargs=...); plain objects passed as task args are pickled per-worker copies, mutations never propagate back.
task: mp.Value shared counter vs pickled list copy

lesson: Pick ThreadPool for IO-bound work (sleep/network releases the GIL) and Pool (processes) for CPU-bound work, since the GIL blocks true multi-core speedup in threads.
task: ThreadPool vs Pool for IO vs CPU work

lesson: Use multiprocessing.shared_memory.SharedMemory with Pool(initializer=...) so each worker attaches to the same named OS buffer by name -- zero-copy, no pickling of array contents each task.
task: SharedMemory zero-copy buffer across Pool workers

lesson: Wrap each pool.imap next() call in try/except: worker exceptions re-raise only at that item's fetch, preserving already-computed results -- unlike pool.map, which discards everything on first failure.
